paragraphs.text: Bundesverband E-Commerce und Versandhandel Deutschland e.V. www.bevh.org
bevh
— Bundesverband E-Commerce und Versandhandel Deutschland e.V. (bevh) —

section_path: Reply to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Berlin, 24 October 2025

section_path: Reply to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Press contacts: Alien Mulyk, alien.mulyk@bevh.org

section_path: Reply to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The Bundesverband E-Commerce und Versandhandel Deutschland e.V. (bevh) represents companies of all sizes and possible forms of trade (online, multichannel, catalogue, TV shopping, platform traders and operators) active in Germany. The members of the bevh account for more than 75 % of total industry turnover. In addition, more than 130 service providers from the e-commerce sector are affiliated to the association.

section_path: Reply to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Customers' trust has always been important for online and distance trading. In principle, companies have a strong interest in making the shopping experience as transparent and positive as possible for customers so that they do not switch to competition. This automatically leads to consumer-friendly behaviour on the part of businesses. Many laws already regulate which guides apply to the design of the shopping experience. The introduction of further provisions is not appropriate. Instead, in order to better protect customers, the EU needs to ensure that the existing legal framework is enforced as uniformly as possible in the Single Market vis-à-vis all actors active in the single market. This is a key factor for fair competition and efficient consumer protection. The planned requirements under the Digital Fairness Act may have far-reaching consequences for the design of websites and elements of customer interaction and retention. This would not lead to more consumer protection, but to double structures, uncertainty and ultimately consumer frustration. For these reasons, we are convinced that the rules provided for in the Digital Fairness Act are neither necessary nor proportionate.

section_path: 1. Better enforcement is the only solution to improve consumer protection and a level playing field
paragraphs.text: In the past legislature, the EU has adopted numerous laws aimed at ensuring high standards of consumer protection and a level playing field for businesses in the Digital Single Market. The lack of coherence between legal acts and differences in implementation by Member States have led to overlaps, fragmentation of the internal market and legal uncertainty. New laws only further exacerbate this problem. Nor will they automatically lead to consumers being exposed to less unfair practices. Instead, there is a need for better and more uniform enforcement of the existing rules. This is the only way to ensure that obligations are applied uniformly and that consumers are effectively protected. Uniform enforcement of consumer protection in all Member States is also a prerequisite for ensuring a level playing field between all actors active in the EU market, whether located inside or outside the EU. The basic conditions for this are a better allocation of resources to the competent authorities, but also the strengthening of cooperation between authorities in the different Member States and at international level. More effective market surveillance, networking and cooperation between authorities and the modernisation of customs are also necessary to prevent unfair competition and to avoid undermining European consumer protection standards.

section_path: 1. Better enforcement is the only solution to improve consumer protection and a level playing field
paragraphs.text: bevh

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
paragraphs.text: There are many examples of how brick-and-mortar shops try to channel consumer behaviour into certain directions. These include the quengel zone at the shop's cash, the route and range of shops, semi-empty shelves and trained sellers. Consumers are aware of these sales techniques and have learned to deal with them. The same applies to online sales, where such practices target consumers in the digital environment. After more than 25 years, it is likely that this will have had a familiarisation effect. A consumer survey carried out in 2024 also shows that consumers are aware and are thinking of shopping online. They do not feel forced to make purchasing decisions and are actively keen not to spend more than they can afford (source bevh (2025), onlinebuyers, n=1003, December 2024).

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
paragraphs.text: This shows that there is no reason to change the consumer's vision and to consider consumers in the digital world no longer as informed and empowered in the future, but more generally as vulnerable. Of course, there are social groups with special needs. However, this is already taken into account, for example, by the Accessibility Reinforcement Act.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
paragraphs.text: A ban on sales practices known from stationery trading for e-commerce alone would discriminate against e-commerce vis-à-vis brick-and-mortar shops. The term 'dark patterns' is a dark pattern in itself, as it suggests a malicious undermining of consumer interest, but this is assessed negatively only because it takes place online. However, it is in the consumer's own interest, for example, to make consumers aware of other products or to display the most relevant search results first. The Commission's plans to combat so-called 'dark patterns' and 'addictive designs' in the Digital Fairness Act may lead to such practices being classified as 'attempting' or 'manipulative' in the future. This would ultimately not lead to more protection, but even to the frustration of customers. Over-regulation that leads to poorer user experience, as in the case of cookie banners, should be avoided. Instead, companies should be allowed to determine how they interact with their customers and design their online shops under the already existing laws. The legislator should respect the freedom to conduct a business and should not impose excessively detailed rules on page design, such as layout, colours or font sizes. There are already a lot of information requirements in e-commerce, while the space available on websites is limited. A completely neutral design of digital sales environments is therefore:

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
paragraphs.text: bevh

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
paragraphs.text: unrealistic, as any presentation of information involves a form of prioritisation. It is therefore important to distinguish between legitimate marketing practices and deceptive design.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
paragraphs.text: In addition to the user-friendliness of online shops and marketplaces, the so-called 'green nudging' could also be a victim of a corresponding rule on dark patterns. For example, traders would no longer be able to encourage consumers to choose

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
paragraphs.text: more sustainable delivery options or shipping packaging or to refrain from returning.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
paragraphs.text: Equal treatment of online and offline trade is central to competitive neutrality and innovation. Only if traders retain room for manoeuvre can there be a dynamic, diverse and consumer-friendly digital ecosystem. What is allowed offline should also be allowed online. There are no grounds for a special scheme. This is because the current principle-based EU legal system is robust and flexible enough to apply to online trade, be it on marketplaces or in webshops. This is the result of a comprehensive study carried out on behalf of Ecommerce Europe, EuroCommerce and Independent Retail Europe by Prof. Jürgen Kühling and Cornelius Sauerborn. This is also recognised in the Guidelines on the interpretation of the UCPD in point 4.2.7. Consumers also enjoy special safeguards when selling online compared to other digital environments, such as social media. They have a full 14-day right of withdrawal. This allows them – even in contrast to brick-and-mortar shops – to have a product if they feel that they do not need it, without giving reasons, to return the product to the distributor. In addition, the Consumer Rights Directive, the UCTD, the GDPR, the AI Act, DSA and DMA also provide many other fair treatment rules for consumers, which are also applicable online.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
paragraphs.text: Introducing a further level of regulation would only increase the complexity of the legislative framework and its enforcement. In particular, many different attempts to define dark patterns are already circulating in existing EU legislation: the Digital Services Act in Recital 67, the AI Act in Article 5, the DMA in Article 13, the Data Act in Recital 38 or the Amendment to Consumer Law 2023. Rather than a new law, it would be much more useful to clarify how these rules fit together. Guidance and guidance for businesses and public authorities on the application and interpretation of existing law would be much more useful. They are faster and more flexible and also better enable companies to apply them to their specific business model.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
paragraphs.text: There is also no need for new "simplified" redress mechanisms. We would point out that it was only with the withdrawal function that a change was made in this regard in online sales. Before resorting to such measures, it should be verified whether they are really necessary and whether they also provide concrete added value to consumers.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
3. Personalised advertising ensures competitiveness
paragraphs.text: Advertising has always been geared towards reaching the right target audience. In the analogue world, this is done, for example, through the correct placement of advertising in certain newspapers, in certain television programmes or on poster walls close to typical locations of the target group. Personalised digital advertising is not to be assessed negatively. It merely develops this long-standing practice.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
3. Personalised advertising ensures competitiveness
paragraphs.text: For businesses, but in particular for SMEs, personalised advertising is already vital today. This will allow them to reach the right target group in the most cost-effective way, given their limited resources. This is particularly important in the current situation of economic stress and in the face of high financial pressure. This is the only way they can remain competitive. As SMEs account for 90% of businesses in the EU, this is ultimately also crucial for the EU's competitiveness, diversity of brands and consumer choice. In particular, with the dissemination of LLMs, AI-based shopping assistants and generative search technologies, new forms of purchasing behaviour are emerging, which require even more individual outreach. This means that personalised advertising is increasingly sought and taken for granted by consumers. This development shows that personalisation is becoming an even more central factor in competition for all companies. Companies that are unable to meet these expectations risk long-term competitiveness and sustainability, including in international comparisons.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
3. Personalised advertising ensures competitiveness
paragraphs.text: The key factor for personalisation is data processing. Already today, consumers have control over their personal data and can easily adapt their settings to refuse personalised advertising if they wish to do so. Personalisation is therefore a matter of data protection rather than consumer protection. Data protection law already sufficiently regulates how and under what conditions data may be used. The Digital Services Act and the Digital Markets Act, as well as the UCPD and the Consumer Rights Directive, also provide tools to address infringements in this area. There is therefore no reason for a new regulation of consumer law.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
3. Personalised advertising ensures competitiveness
paragraphs.text: bevh

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
4. No new rules needed for influencer marketing
paragraphs.text: There is also no need for new rules in the area of influencer marketing. The current legal framework, consisting of the provisions of product safety law, consumer law and the Digital Services Act, also applies to social commerce and influencer marketing. Key issues such as requirements on advertising labelling, brand responsibility, dealing with illegal products, deceptive practices and the protection of minors are already covered. Instead of new legislation, it would be more appropriate to provide practical and easy-to-understand guidelines for influencers and brands, to strengthen the enforcement of existing laws and to raise consumer awareness.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
5. Avoid confusion in terms of prices
paragraphs.text: In the area of price indications, a distinction must also be drawn between the practices envisaged by the Commission and those which are non-discriminatory, legitimate and even used by consumers. These include, for example, offers referring to different products of a particular category in a certain price range, such as 'cheeks of less than EUR 50' or 'pasts of EUR 30 or more'. Moreover, in online sales, the total price can often only be indicated in the checkout because the composition and size of the final basket or the delivery speed chosen by the customer, for example, determine the delivery costs to be paid. Even in the case of personalised products, the final price cannot be indicated in advance. If the consumer can choose different dimensions, colours and materials for a product, it makes more sense for the consumer to adapt the price dynamically to the characteristics of the product chosen by the consumer. If all prices for all possible product compositions were displayed to the consumer at once, this would be much more confusing.

section_path: 2. No need for new rules on website design
5. Avoid confusion in terms of prices
paragraphs.text: Discounts and price comparisons are already covered by the UCPD and the PID. The different interpretations in the Member States have led to different applications by national authorities. Clarifying guidance from the European Commission, which allows traders to provide meaningful information to consumers while limiting fragmentation of interpretation, can be helpful. This requires that the guidelines strengthen legal certainty rather than create new bureaucratic hurdles. The aim should be to promote the most consistent, workable and consumer-friendly application of existing rules. What matters is not with which price (the previous price or other reference prices) a trader compares a price, but that the nature of the comparison is clear and not misleading. The Commission should also clarify in its guidelines which products are covered by the exemption, when and for how long. More clarity also contributes to better implementation and enforcement.
